title: Persistent fevers with a new murmur

chart.oneLiner: 45M presents with 3 weeks of intermittent fevers, night sweats, fatigue, and weight loss; a new heart murmur is noted.

opening: I've had fevers and night sweats for weeks, I'm wiped out, and I've lost weight.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 45-year-old man, chronically unwell-appearing, fatigued. Good historian.

HPI: 3 weeks of intermittent fevers, drenching night sweats, malaise, and ~8 lb weight loss. On asking: a history of injection drug use (or a recent dental procedure / known valve disease) — only if asked about drug use, dental work, or heart history. NO localizing symptoms initially. May report transient neurologic symptoms or back pain (embolic phenomena) if asked.
PMH: possible prior valve disease. MEDS: none regular. SOCIAL: injection drug use (only if asked).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: ill-appearing, febrile.
- Vitals: BP 118/64, HR 100, RR 18, 38.5°C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Cardiac: a NEW regurgitant murmur (e.g., blowing systolic/diastolic) if auscultated.
- Peripheral stigmata if examined: splinter hemorrhages under the nails, Janeway lesions (painless palms/soles), Osler nodes (painful finger pads), conjunctival/Roth spots.
- Splenomegaly if examined. Skin: track marks if examined.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer injection drug use, dental work, or valve history unless asked about drug use, recent procedures, or heart history.
- The murmur, peripheral stigmata, splenomegaly, and track marks are revealed only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.BloodCultures: THREE sets of blood cultures from separate sites drawn BEFORE antibiotics; growing gram-positive cocci (e.g., Staph aureus).
labs.CBC: Anemia of chronic disease; mild leukocytosis.
labs.Echo: Echocardiogram (TTE then TEE): valvular vegetation with associated regurgitation; assess for abscess.
labs.Duke: Findings applied to the modified Duke criteria.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia; a NEW PR prolongation / conduction delay would suggest a perivalvular (aortic root) abscess — an important complication to look for.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'First Degree AV Block' library page — download an example tracing that matches: Sinus tachycardia; a NEW PR prolongation / conduction delay would suggest a perivalvular (aortic root) abscess. Self-host with credit to litfl.com.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: May show multiple peripheral nodular opacities (septic pulmonary emboli) in right-sided endocarditis; otherwise may be clear.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC chest film showing multiple nodular opacities (septic emboli) OR a normal film; label which it is.
cxr.imageDescription: May show multiple peripheral nodular opacities (septic pulmonary emboli) in right-sided endocarditis; otherwise may be clear.
searchTerms: chest radiograph show multiple peripheral nodular

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Infective endocarditis (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Occult bacteremia/sepsis from another source (CANT-MISS,3). Malignancy/lymphoma (core,3). Tuberculosis or other chronic infection (core,2). Autoimmune/vasculitis (core,2). Connects prolonged fever + new murmur + risk factor (IVDU/valve) + embolic stigmata to endocarditis (core,6).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Infective endocarditis. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Occult bacteremia/sepsis from another source. Also reasonable: Malignancy/lymphoma; Tuberculosis or other chronic infection; Autoimmune/vasculitis.

coreActions.item: Connects prolonged fever + new murmur + risk factor (IVDU/valve) + embolic stigmata to endocarditis

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order? (Order matters here.)
steps.rubric: At least 3 sets of blood cultures from separate sites, ideally BEFORE antibiotics in a stable patient (CANT-MISS,5). Echocardiogram — TTE then TEE (CANT-MISS,4). CBC, ESR/CRP (2). Urinalysis (1). EKG to look for conduction block/abscess (2). Apply the modified Duke criteria (1). PENALTY: starting antibiotics before drawing cultures in a stable patient (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Blood cultures; CBC; Inflammatory markers; Urinalysis; Echo; Duke.

criticalActions.item: At least 3 sets of blood cultures from separate sites, ideally BEFORE antibiotics in a stable patient

penalties.item: starting antibiotics before drawing cultures in a stable patient

requiredTests: At least 3 sets of blood cultures from separate sites, ideally BEFORE antibiotics in a stable patient
contraindicatedTests: starting antibiotics before drawing cultures in a stable patient
orderSensitiveActions: starting antibiotics before drawing cultures in a stable patient

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings (look for a clue to a complication).
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia ± new PR prolongation. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (5); identifies PR prolongation/conduction delay if present (5); recognizes a NEW conduction abnormality suggests a perivalvular/aortic-root abscess (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: missing the conduction clue (-2).
keyFindings: identifies PR prolongation/conduction delay if present
recognizes a NEW conduction abnormality suggests a perivalvular/aortic-root abscess
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia; a NEW PR prolongation / conduction delay would suggest a perivalvular (aortic root) abscess — an important complication to look for.
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia; a NEW PR prolongation / conduction delay would suggest a perivalvular (aortic root) abscess — an important complication to look for.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Sinus tachycardia; a NEW PR prolongation / conduction delay would suggest a perivalvular (aortic root) abscess — an important complication to look for.

coreActions.item: identifies PR prolongation/conduction delay if present
recognizes a NEW conduction abnormality suggests a perivalvular/aortic-root abscess

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = multiple nodular opacities (septic emboli) in right-sided IE, or clear. CREDIT: identifies nodular opacities/septic emboli OR a normal film (6); links nodules to septic pulmonary emboli in right-sided endocarditis (4). PENALTY: inventing unrelated pathology (-2).
keyFindings: identifies nodular opacities/septic emboli OR a normal film
links nodules to septic pulmonary emboli in right-sided endocarditis
criticalFindings: May show multiple peripheral nodular opacities (septic pulmonary emboli) in right-sided endocarditis; otherwise may be clear.
steps.idealAnswer: May show multiple peripheral nodular opacities (septic pulmonary emboli) in right-sided endocarditis; otherwise may be clear.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: May show multiple peripheral nodular opacities (septic pulmonary emboli) in right-sided endocarditis; otherwise may be clear.

coreActions.item: identifies nodular opacities/septic emboli OR a normal film
links nodules to septic pulmonary emboli in right-sided endocarditis

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Positive blood cultures, a vegetation on echo, fever, and a new murmur. What is your diagnosis and framework?
steps.rubric: Infective endocarditis (6). Applies the modified Duke criteria — major (positive cultures, echo vegetation) plus minor criteria (fever, predisposition, vascular/immunologic phenomena) (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Infective endocarditis, diagnosed by the modified Duke criteria: two major criteria are met — persistently positive blood cultures with a typical organism and a vegetation with regurgitation on echo — supported by minor criteria including fever, a predisposition (injection drug use/valve disease), and vascular/immunologic phenomena (Janeway lesions, Osler nodes, Roth spots, hematuria).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH empiric antibiotics (endocarditis): obtain ≥3 sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics (CANT-MISS,5); empiric vancomycin + ceftriaxone (native) (CANT-MISS,6); echocardiography TTE→TEE (4); early ID consult (4); assess surgical indications – HF/abscess/persistent bacteremia (3); evaluate embolic foci (2). PENALTY: starting antibiotics before cultures in a stable patient (-3); MSSA treated with vancomycin when beta-lactam available (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Obtain ≥3 sets of blood cultures from separate sites before antibiotics. Empiric therapy for native-valve IE: vancomycin + ceftriaxone (prosthetic: add gentamicin + rifampin – ask ID). Echocardiography (TTE then TEE). Early Infectious Disease consult improves mortality. Evaluate for embolic/metastatic foci and indications for surgery (HF, abscess, persistent bacteremia, large vegetation).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 45M presents with 3 weeks of intermittent fevers, night sweats, fatigue, and weight loss; a new heart murmur is noted. The diagnosis is Infective endocarditis, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Splenomegaly if examined. Skin: track marks if examined.
Cardiac: a NEW regurgitant murmur (e.g., blowing systolic/diastolic) if auscultated.
Splenomegaly if examined. Skin: track marks if examined.
Peripheral stigmata if examined: splinter hemorrhages under the nails, Janeway lesions (painless palms/soles), Osler nodes (painful finger pads), conjunctival/Roth spots.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Infective endocarditis while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Infective endocarditis and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
